data.title: Off-Strip Domination: Why Red Rock Is My Favorite Hotel And Casino In Vegas
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Red Rock Resorts (NYSE:RRR) is poised for significant growth, driven by its undervalued real estate portfolio, which includes expansive developed and undeveloped properties in the rapidly expanding Las Vegas local market. The company's gaming-centric business model, considered best-in-class, allows it to dominate the second-largest U.S. gaming market with the largest market share among locals. Led by an experienced management team, Red Rock is well-positioned to capitalize on its operational efficiency and untapped development opportunities. With strong growth potential and underappreciated assets, RRR represents a compelling investment opportunity.

Business Overview

Red Rock Resorts develops and operates casino and entertainment properties in the United States, primarily through two segments, Las Vegas Operations and Native American Management. They own and operate 6 gaming and entertainment facilities and 12 smaller casinos in the Las Vegas regional market. The principal source of revenue is gaming at 67%, where 80-85% of casino revenue is generated from slot play. Their closest competitor, Caesars, is at 27%. Red Rock continues to create value and succeed through its organic growth pipelines. They hold over 522 acres in a land bank valued at over $1B. Red Rock has 100% ownership of all development real estate, and all historic greenfield properties developed by RRR have produced a 20%+ IRR.

Earnings

RRR posted Q2 earnings where net revenues were $486.4mm, a 16.9% increase from Q2 2023. Property EBITDA was up 15% YoY with the main source being the newly developed Durango property, which opened in early December 2023. In Q2 the Durango property increased visitation by 88% while gaining 55,000 new customers to its database. Durango is on track to become one of RRR’s highest EBITDA margin properties. Management noted they experienced positive YoY growth as they targeted “high performance in its VIP section,” which was an intentional move to attract a higher network customer alongside its regional and national targets.

Unit Economics

My investment thesis is predicated on three main points. First, Red Rock has pure-play exposure to the attractive Las Vegas local market, with a pole position to benefit from growth in a more affluent population. More than 70% of the population residing in Clark County (population of 2.3mm) is located within 3 miles of a Red Rock-owned property or greenfield site. This is based on steady, high-quality demand growth, driven by steady population growth skewing to higher-income, higher-net-worth individuals seeking a lower tax rate and a lower cost of living. RRR assets are of higher quality than any of their peers because of their geographic position, they can use this as pricing power and can use it as a lever for revenue growth. The revenue for the entire Las Vegas strip is $7.9B and the local market is $3B. Nevada law (SB 208) significantly limits the development of casinos off the famed Strip and Red Rock occupies most of that real estate. I believe this is why Red Rock has sustained superior growth relative to its peers over the years.

In Las Vegas, there is a low-risk/high-return development pipeline that provides an attractive growth runaway. RRR's undeveloped land bank is underappreciated, as land sale comps don't give credit to the irreplaceable nature of the attached (scarce) gaming licenses coupled with their strategic locations. Its newest property Durango, opened in December 2023. Durango's success will re-cement management credibility with new-build development and offer investors a blueprint to value RRR's strategically positioned land bank. Projections for Durango (Phase 1) adding $145m in EBITDA in 2025, an 18% lift to the existing portfolio, while Phase 2 of Durango could boost the property's returns by 40%.

With Las Vegas being cheaper in terms of cost of living and #1 for new corporate investment in the U.S., Red Rock is in the best position to take advantage of this. Red Rock has a superior capital structure with an interest coverage of 4.48x demonstrating that the company can endure a high-rate environment with current income levels. With a 19% ROIC, they have sustained a consistent 40% EBITDA margin for the last 15 quarters.

Personal income in the Las Vegas Metropolitan area is projected to have reached $145B in 2023; incomes expanded by 6.4% CAGR during the past decade and 5.3% during the past 20 years. This positive tailwind and growth in Las Vegas has allowed Red Rock to invest in its off-strip properties and capitalize on this underserved market.

Growth Strategy

Red Rock’s main growth driver in its pipeline is their North Folk development. RRR has acquired 305 acres adjacent to Highway 99 between Fresno, CA and Madera, CA with the capacity to host 81,000 vehicles per day. 5.8 million people currently live within 2 hours of this site and Red Rock plans to house 2478 slots and 42 table games at this property. It is important to note that the first phase of this development does not involve a hotel. RRR will receive a 30% management fee on net revenue and a 4% development fee for this property. There were negotiations with the Tribe about this development, however, this has been resolved and RRR can move forward with the project. There are favorable tax implications without a negotiated compact (contract between two or more states) and this will directly benefit margins. The 30% management fee Red Rock will collect will roughly contribute to 40% of the property's EBITDA. I believe this is where RRR separates itself. Its business model travels very well and can attack the local markets better than any competitor while sustaining high EBITDA margins.

Risks

The first risk is that a macro (recession) pullback threatens to derail domestic demand. Persistent levels of heightened inflation pose a risk to consumer demand for discretionary spending. Increased costs to the consumer for staples (gas, rent, food) pressure their ability to spend on leisure activities. However, with recent rate cuts and inflation cooling, I don’t see this as a major risk to operations.

The second risk is regarding changes to Las Vegas restrictions on new gaming enterprises could pressure existing operators. Any changes that ease the existing restrictions on new gaming capacity in the local market could garner new supply into the area, and likely negatively impact existing properties for operators such as RRR.

Finally, labor inflation could weigh on margins, while land-based promotions could return to some extent. Elevated levels of wage inflation pose a risk to the heightened margins seen in the gaming industry since operators re-opened their doors from pandemic-related shutdowns. Although, with a 2-3-year period since the pandemic, this hasn’t been a concern for management.

Valuation

I found my cost of equity using the CAPM model accompanied by the 30-year treasury bill as my risk-free rate and added it to the beta of 1.05. I multiplied it by the equity risk premium, which is the TTM S&P 500 return subtracted from my 30-year treasury bill risk-free rate.

The cost of debt of 5% was pulled from Bloomberg.

To calculate my WACC, I multiplied them by the % of equity and % of debt in the capital structure to arrive at 8.2% as my discount rate.

Revenue projections are in line with consensus estimates, which slow, steady growth over the years to come. There is some compression in the later years of the model acting as a conservative approach to how new development properties will perform once complete. RRR has proven to maintain strong EBITDA margins, however, I wanted to illustrate that if margins were to compress about 5% from the 40%+ margins, there is still upside to be had. I forecast current liabilities will see a significant increase with the debt put on the company from the North Folk and the final stages of project Durango. Sticking to this theme, analysts have RRR’s EV/EBITDA at 10.5x however I wanted to stay more in line with peers in the 7-9x ballpark.

Conclusion

In summary, Red Rock Resorts (NASDAQ:RRR) offers strong market positioning and substantial growth potential. Its strategic land bank, well-managed real estate portfolio, and market supremacy among Las Vegas locals offer a strong basis for future growth. RRR's distinct assets, operational prowess, and strong balance sheet position it to outperform its competitors and generate significant value for investors as the Las Vegas market grows.

data.title: DiamondRock Hospitality: Wait For A Dip In The 8.25% Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

DiamondRock Hospitality (NYSE:DRH) is a hotel REIT owning 36 properties with almost 10,000 rooms across the United States. About a third of its EBITDA was generated in Chicago, Boston and New York City, and interestingly, DiamondRock prefers its hotels to be independently managed rather than belonging to one of the larger brands. This reduces the guest flow from guests loyal to certain brands and loyalty programs but it also allows for a different pricing mechanism.

In my previous articles I had a closer look at the REIT’s preferred shares and the preferred equity also is the focus of this article as the share price of the preferred shares is trading at about 4% above the call price, with the initial call date coming pretty close.

The hotel business is still doing fine with an anticipated $200M+ in AFFO this year

As I’m looking at DiamondRock Hotels from the perspective of a preferred shareholder, I will focus on two specific elements: Are the preferred dividends well covered, and is there a major balance sheet risk involved with owning (senior) equity in the REIT?

A good starting point is the FFO and AFFO result generated by the REIT. As you can see below, DiamondRock posted total FFO of $50.1M in the second quarter of this year, while the adjusted FFO was approximately $72.5M as there were quite a few non-recurring items that negatively impacted the reported results.

This means the AFO increased by almost 8% in the second quarter of the year while the increase was just 3% in the first semester as the first quarter is traditionally relatively weak.

The hotel REIT also published its full-year guidance, and as you can see below, it expects to generate a full-year AFFO of $201.5M-$213.5M. Keep in mind the starting point of the FFO and AFFO calculation and guidance is the net income, and as shown below (and above), it already takes the preferred dividend payments into account. DiamondRock expects to pay $9.82M in preferred dividends this year, which means it needs less than 5% of the pre-dividend AFFO to cover the preferred dividend payments.

So from the perspective of the dividend coverage ratio, I don’t see any issues. And looking at the balance sheet, you see there’s $1.64B in equity on the balance sheet, of which $119% is preferred equity.

This means there's in excess of $1.5B in common equity, which ranks junior to the preferred shares. This also provides a decent safety net.

I consider the preferred shares a term investment

DiamondRock only has one series of preferred shares outstanding, a cumulative issue, offering an annual preferred dividend of $2.0625 per preferred share paid in four quarterly installments. The securities, trading with (NYSE:DRH.PR.A) as ticker symbol, can be called from Aug. 31 next year on. And as I'm expecting interest rates in the financial markets to decrease between now and the summer of next year, I think it’s safe to say we should probably consider an investment in DiamondRock as a 10.5-month term investment. That being said, there's of course absolutely no guarantee DiamondRock will call these preferred shares in August next year, but the longer they wait, the higher the annualized return will be as the current premium will be amortized over a longer period.

As you can see in the image above, the current share price of DiamondRock preferred shares is $25.92, which means it's trading at a premium of almost 4% to the call price of $25/share.

The preferred shares just paid a preferred dividend on Sept. 30, which means an investor should be able to expect three more quarterly preferred dividend payments as well as a pro rata dividend payment for two months. This means an investor can expect to receive at least $1.71875 in payments on top of the repayment of the $25 principal resulting in a total payment of $26.7185. That’s a total return of 3.08% on the current investment for a 10.5 month period. Even on an annualized basis, the return on the preferred shares would be lower than what an investor could earn on a one-year US Treasury Bill (currently 4.21% per year).

Of course the scenario above solely focuses on DiamondRock calling the preferred shares at the first opportunity it has. If the preferred shares would only be called in the summer of 2026, the yield to call would increase to approximately 6% assuming a call in June 2026.

Investment thesis

This means that if you believe the preferred shares will be called in August 2025, selling the preferred shares would be the right call (subject of course to transaction fees and the impact of taxes) as the total return until the call date is pretty low. However, if you anticipate the preferred shares will remain outstanding beyond August 2025, it definitely makes sense to hold on to the position as the capital loss upon a call can be amortized over a longer period.

The common shares of DiamondRock are also still relatively interesting, but I have no position in those common shares. I also have no position in the preferred shares anymore.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: Undervalued And Thriving In The Post-3G Era
data.description:
There's wisdom in buying and holding on to companies with household brands that resonate with consumers. Perhaps that's why Warren Buffett's portfolio in Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) is chock-full of strong brands that have stood the test of time.

This brings me to Kraft Heinz (NASDAQ:KHC), which remains a top holding in Berkshire Hathaway. As of reporting in August, Buffett still holds 326 million shares in KHC, and unlike his recent culling of shares in Apple (AAPL) and Bank of America (BAC), he did not sell a single share of KHC in the second quarter.

I last covered KHC back in July, highlighting its supply chain efficiencies, price increases to offset margin pressure, and material undervaluation. It appears that the market has agreed to my thesis, with the stock giving investors a 14% total return since my last piece, which is double the 7% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe.

The recent 50 basis point reduction in interest rates by the Federal Reserve may have given presumed slower growth stocks like those in consumer staples a bid, as lower rates make their dividends more appealing. Since my last piece, the SPDR Consumer Staples ETF (XLP) has outperformed the S&P 500, and KHC has outperformed even XLP with a 12.5% share price appreciation, as shown below.

In this article, I revisit KHC including recent business performance and discuss why it remains a solid pick for potentially strong long-term returns for income and value investors, so let's get started!

Why KHC?

Kraft Heinz is one of the largest food companies in the world, with a presence in 190 countries and a host of iconic brands in Heinz Ketchup, Kraft Mac & Cheese and condiments, Oscar Mayer, Philadelphia cream cheese, and more.

Those who follow the company know that outsized influence that the Brazilian private equity firm, 3G Capital, had over KHC starting in 2015, when it and Berkshire Hathaway merged the Heinz Company with Kraft. In typical PE fashion, 3G sought to cut costs in order to squeeze higher profits, but that came at the expense of a lack of brand innovation.

3G began pulling back from its ownership stake in 2019 and in 2023 exited its remaining 16% stake in the company. At the same time, KHC has been ramping investments behind its brands to reinvigorate growth. This strategy appears to be working, as KHC's volume declines have slowed down for 4 consecutive quarters. As shown below, KHC's volume decline would be -2.5% had it not been for the impact from temporary plant closure and a rebranding effort currently underway on Lunchables.

While total volume did decline by 3.4% during Q2 2024, as shown above, KHC was able to largely offset that with price increases, resulting in revenue declining by just 0.5% YoY during the quarter. Also encouraging, KHC is seeing growth in gross margins through operational efficiencies. This is reflected by adjusted gross margin rising by 190 basis points on a YoY basis to 35.0% for the first half of this year.

Higher margins contributed to 9% YoY growth in free cash flow to $100 million during the second quarter. This helped to enable $1.5 billion in capital returns to shareholders during the first half of this year, including $969 million worth of dividends paid and $350 million in share repurchases. KHC initiated its share buyback program in Q4 of last year, and over the past 3 reported quarters, has retired a meaningful 1.5% of the outstanding float.

Going forward, management seeks to close the gap with competitors from a pricing perspective and plans to make investments behind 30-40% of its brands. This includes brand innovation, such as the one behind the recent launch of new Mac & Cheese products with different shapes and flavors. Innovations also include partnerships with Taco Bell and Delimex to offer new Mexican-inspired meal solutions to drive sustained long-term growth. Also importantly, KHC is expanding its Away from Home category by improving service levels and expanded distribution.

Looking ahead to Q3 results, I would look for an update on the Away from Home category, as management expects to build momentum during the second half of this year. Moreover, I'd like to see progress toward innovation around the Lunchables and Capri Sun categories, both of which have been in the headlines as of late due to an increasingly health-conscious environment regarding processed foods and packaging. Recent developments include better brand positioning and product evolution, which includes the introduction of a bottle form of Capri Sun.

Meanwhile, KHC continues to deleverage the balance sheet with the aforementioned strong free cash flow generation, reducing the adjusted debt-to-equity ratio by 0.1x on a YoY basis to a 3.0x level, which is on par with a safe level generally considered safe by ratings agencies, supporting its BBB investment grade credit rating.

Importantly for income investors, KHC yields 4.4% and the dividend is well-covered by a 54% payout ratio. While dividend growth has been lacking as of late, share repurchases seem to be a good use of capital returns at the current discounted price of $36.00 with a forward PE of 11.9.

I continue to find KHC to be good value at the current price, given my expectations that the company will return to meaningful growth as it moves past its "3G era" of cost-cutting and low brand investments, with new brand innovation growing by 60 bps as a percentage of sales to 2.4% since the start of the year.

With a well-covered 4.4% yield and sell-side analysts estimates for 5% to 7% annual EPS growth over the next 3 years, KHC could reasonably deliver market-level total returns over the medium term. Over the long-run, I would expect for KHC to be able to trade at a 15-18x PE valuation, which is in-line with that of strong consumer staples stocks as it continues to make progress toward deleveraging the balance sheet.

Risks to the thesis include higher-than-expected cost inflation, which could negatively impact KHC's margins should it not be able to keep up with price increases. Moreover, competition from private label brands remains a potential headwind, as that introduces pricing pressure. Moreover, an increasingly health-conscious consumer base could mean that rebranding efforts on items like Lunchables may fall flat with consumers and not generate the desired growth.

Investor Takeaway

Kraft Heinz remains a solid investment opportunity for income and value investors, supported by a well-covered 4.4% dividend yield and a strong focus on brand innovation and operational efficiency post-3G Capital's cost-cutting era.

Despite modest volume declines, KHC has managed to offset this through strategic price increases and improved margins, with a high single-digit rise in free cash flow and robust capital returns to shareholders. With a forward P/E of 11.9, a commitment to deleveraging, and estimated 5-7% EPS growth by analysts over the next three years, KHC is well-positioned for potentially strong long-term total returns from the current price.

data.title: Avista: Slightly Undervalued, Significant Leverage And Better Protection With This Strategy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In a previous article about Evergy (EVRG) I explained the reasons why six to nine months ago I became increasingly interested in utilities: expectations for lower interest rates (process that has already started) and increased demand of electricity due to AI, electric vehicles and other sources.

And while utilities already had a significant jump in the meantime, we can still find some interesting cases. For regulated utilities, I look for undervalued stocks with a combined dividend yield plus dividend growth of at least 8%.

One of the companies that qualifies is Avista Corporation (NYSE:AVA), a regulated electric and natural gas utility with a dividend yield of about 5% and a dividend growth close to 4%. That is slightly different from Evergy, which offers a dividend yield a bit over 4% and dividend growth close to 5%.

Adding a slight undervaluation to the equation, and the stock might be interesting as a defensive position in a diversified portfolio. For these cases of slightly undervalued low volatility stocks, I prefer a Bull Call (or Put) Spread options strategy because it brings significant leverage and better protection to the downside.

Business Analysis and Economic Moat

Avista is involved in the production, transmission and distribution of energy and some other marginal energy-related businesses. They claim to be one of the cleanest utilities when it comes to greenhouse gases, balancing reliability with renewable energy sources, while managing costs for customers.

The company provides electricity and natural gas to some areas spread across several states:

  • Washington, where they have a pending multi-year electric and natural gas rate case filed in January 2024.
  • Idaho, where they have an approved settlement designed to increase annual base electric revenues by 8%, effective in September 2023, and by 1.7% effective in September 2024; for natural gas they have an agreed increase of 2.7% effective in September 2023.
  • Oregon, where they have an approved settlement designed to increase annual base natural gas revenues by 9.4% effective January 2024.
  • Alaska, where they have an order which results in an approved base electric revenue increase of 6%, effective in October 2023.

The number of electric retail customers increased YoY in the first six months ending June 2024 by about 1.3%, while the number of natural gas retail customers increased in the same period by about 0.8%, based especially on the residential segment. Population growth in Idaho is a contributing factor and is expected to continue the ascending trend through 2029. Oregon also fares well with estimated population growth until 2030.

While the number of commercial and industrial customers is relatively flat YoY, the electric revenues from these segments did increase, helped by the new rates. I also think we could see positive surprises from these segments based on the increased demand of electricity I was talking about in the introduction.

Based on the above, we can conclude that Avista, as most regulated utilities, has an economic moat with the source of the monopoly that they have in certain areas. But again, we can say that this moat is only a weak (or narrow) one, a powerful moat being very difficult due to the limits that the regulators impose on them.

Financial Analysis and Dividend

Avista's financial position is in good shape, with about 5.4x Net Debt / EBITDA. With a BBB credit rating, negative outlook from S&P Global, they score a notch under the average for US regulated utilities, and their interest coverage is a little under the average. However, I think it's possible to see some positive evolutions based on the last rate base increases and lower interest rates.

According to their latest earnings presentation, they expect a rate base growth of 5% though 2026, and I expect that to be continued based on the increased demand of electricity:

In terms of profitability, I think we can expect between 4% and 6% annual EPS growth through 2026, depending on various factors like their pending rate case in Washington, increased residential customers, amplitude of interest rate cuts. Positive surprises might come from the commercial and industrial segments. For example, here is an announcement from the last earnings report:

Effective Aug. 1, we added a large electric customer in our service territory previously served by the wholesale markets. The increase in utility margin due to the addition of this customer is expected to offset substantially all of the forecast impact of higher power supply costs on results in 2024.

The dividend has a current yield of about 5%, but dividend growth is not so strong: ~4.5% in 2023 and ~3.3% in 2024. I expect the growth next years to stay in this range, due to a relatively elevated payout ratio at about 77%, considering their estimated 2024 EPS of 2.46 at the midpoint.

Fair Value

Let's see where Avista is positioned against some comparable regulated US utilities:

Looking at Price / Book, one of my favorite multiples for regulated utilities, with 1.18 Avista has more than 40% discount compared to the average. That's partially explained by their Return on Equity = ~7.84%, about 20% lower than peers, but the difference indicates undervaluation. The EV / EBITDA multiple has again a discount of about 13% versus peers.

But since this comparison cannot say anything if the entire industry is over or under-valued, let's take a look at Avista's five years averages: Price / Book = 1.33, about 12% more than today, and EV / EBITDA = 11.95, about the same 12% more than today.

For an absolute valuation, I use a DDM (Discounted Dividend Model) in two stages: about five years with higher dividend growth, then a lower dividend growth in perpetuity. Here are my assumptions for Avista:

Compared with the current stock price (37.98), the DDM shows an undervaluation of about 13%, which is in line with the relative valuation.

In conclusion, this is what Avista could bring us from a stock position:

  • 12-13% return from the discount to fair value (this has a degree of uncertainty, of course, even if it's low for a regulated utility)
  • ~5% dividend yield
  • 3 to 4% dividend growth

While this is not shy for a defensive position, I look at options for both better returns and better protection. If some investors, for any reason, don't want to get involved with options, they can skip the next section.

Significant Leverage and Better Protection Through Options

Like for Evergy, I chose a Bull Call (or Put) Spread strategy. There are several reasons why this strategy: the stock is slightly undervalued, but without significant potential, while this strategy brings significant leverage; low volatility makes selling options less appealing; limited loss profile compared to stocks or other strategies. For beginners completely unfamiliar with options, I do encourage them to read, but I recommend testing this strategy first on a demo account for about one year.

For a Bull Call Spread, if the short Call is In-The-Money with very low extrinsic value, we could be assigned just before the ex-dividend date, which translates to short 100 shares instead of a short Call, which will cost us about $49 dividend. While this is a manageable situation (like I described in my article about Evergy), since Call Spreads and Put Spreads have a similar profile, let's see a Bull Put Spread in this case.

For a Bull Put spread, we could still be assigned exactly after the ex-dividend date if the short Put is In-The-Money with low extrinsic value, which translates to long 100 shares instead of short Put. But that's not very problematic because we could immediately close the position, with minor changes regarding the profit/loss.

For example, we can sell an AVA 40 Put Mar '25 for about $325, and we can buy an AVA 35 Put for about $105. Total cost is 500 (the spread between the Puts, which is the maximum cash that I need to pay at expiration if both Puts finish In-The-Money) - 220 (difference between premiums that I collected) = about $280.

The maximum profit at expiration would be about $220 for stock price above $40, meaning 78.5%, or about 188% annualized. The break-even is at about $37.8, and maximum loss is the total cost for stock price under $35.

Let's see how we can handle this position in different scenarios to profit even at lower prices:

Scenario 1: Stock price above $40 (this is my base case considering expectations for lower interest rates in about six months, that's why I chose March 2025 as expiration date):

Let the position collect the maximum profit, that could be ~188% annualized at expiration or even higher if we close it before. For example, if we could close the position in February 2025 for $15, it's not worth it to keep it more (we avoid a possible price reversal), and we can gather a profit of (220 - 15) / 280 = about 73%, or 219% annualized.

Scenario 2: Stock price retreats to low thirties in 2-3 months, then it recovers only about half the loss next year, settling at about $35 (possible if interest rate cuts are delayed for a few months):

It's better to close the position while our long 35 Put is at around $35 and still has extrinsic value, 2-3 months before expiration. Otherwise, with stock price under $35 at expiration, we'll get the maximum loss as in the chart above.

In a second iteration, with the stock price under $35, we could open another Put Spread at lower strikes, short 35 Put, long 30 Put, expiration June '25, which is very similar to our initial position, maybe at a slightly higher cost.

Then, with stock price at around $35, in a third iteration we can just roll over the same position (short 35 Put, long 30 Put):
