In this scenario, the total cost (maximum funds needed to be set aside) is a bit higher since we need extra funds after the loss in the first iteration in order to open the second iteration, but in about one year we will still have a significant return of about 195/460 = 42.3%, and that even if the stock price is lower, at $35!

Scenario 3: The stock price retreats to the low thirties in 2–3 months, then it keeps heading down to under $30 (not very likely, but still possible scenario in case of higher interest rates for much longer):

The second iteration described above will finish with another loss, we'll have a total loss of about $300.

Now, with the stock in the high twenties, Avista would be significantly undervalued, and we could switch to a more aggressive strategy, to counterbalance the effect of this combined loss and, in most cases, still bring us a consistent profit. Buying a Call option is such a strategy, but contrary to some popular opinions that a Call (or Put) Spread is more difficult (because it has two legs compared to one for a single Call), in fact, buying a Call is a more difficult strategy to implement because there are several intricacies, so that might be a subject for another article. Or even owning the stock is almost always a good choice when it's significantly undervalued.

Finally, even in the very unlikely scenario of Avista heading to bankruptcy in 2–3 months, we still want the Put (or Call) spread due to its limited loss profile, while owning the stock would lead to much higher losses.

Risks

The obvious interest rate risk is mitigated, or sometimes nullified, by the active management of the Put Spread before expiration and the position rollover, like I described above in Scenario 2 and Scenario 3.

Extreme weather can provoke some damage to utilities. While the US West Coast is not very exposed to hurricanes, there is an increasing risk of wildfires due to higher temperatures. Idaho is one of the states with a high risk of wildfires, especially from mid-summer to autumn. But Avista is taking active measures, one of their top 3 capital programs 2024-2026 is "Wildfire resiliency", as presented above. Their subsidiary, Alaska Electric & Power, faces the risk of avalanches, and they are implementing an avalanche diversion project to mitigate that risk.

Related to the Put Spread strategy, Avista Put options are not very liquid. If someone wants to open a large number of Put spreads, there can be problems regarding the spread, so higher costs. In this case, switching to the similar Call spread strategy might help, I see that Open Interest is higher for Calls than for Puts. If there are still problems with getting decent prices, opening several smaller positions instead of a big one not only that alleviates this, but also can offer even better protection because we could add new Call (or Put) spread positions at better prices if the stock price moves down.

Takeaway

Avista, like most utilities, will benefit from lower interest rates and from an increased demand of electricity. With regulated electricity and natural gas operations in Washington, Idaho, Oregon, Alaska, the company has a weak (or narrow) economic moat.

They are targeting 5% rate base growth through 2026, and I expect a 4 to 6% EPS growth. The dividend yield is 5%, but with a relatively high payout ratio of about 77%, I expect lower annual dividend growth, likely between 3 and 4%.

Avista seems 12-13% undervalued, based on both a relative valuation and an absolute one. A two-stages Discounted Dividend Model (with 3.75% dividend growth next 5 years, 3% growth in perpetuity and 7.7% Discount rate) gives a Fair Value = 43.05 vs. 37.98 stock price today.

For significant leverage and better protection, I chose a bullish 40/35 Put spread strategy. The strategy could bring close to 200% annualized return with stock price over $40, or about 42% return even with the price retreating towards low thirties in 2-3 months, and then settling at ~$35. If the price continued to head down, I would recommend switching to a more aggressive strategy at that moment (because the stock would be significantly undervalued at those levels) to counter-balance the initial losses. Even in an unlikely scenario of Avista heading to bankruptcy, this strategy is much better than simply owning the stock, due to its limited loss profile.

data.title: Celestica: Riding The Wave Of Unstoppable AI Momentum
data.description:
Introduction

I am pleased to report that Celestica's (NYSE:CLS) (TSX:CLS:CA) share price grew by 40% since I covered it in April for the first time. I hope this was beneficial for readers who decided to opt in based on my call.

Celestica is an interesting stock and definitely deserves a thesis update, especially considering the upcoming earnings release which is planned for October 23. Tailwinds remain very strong for Celestica, as several recent developments suggest that spending for data centers is still very strong, and this momentum is not getting weaker. If recent months showed us that only tech giants were investing vast amounts in building new data centers, now we see some prominent asset managers also expanding into this field. Celestica has been strong so far in absorbing AI tailwinds, and I see no reasons for this trend to terminate. My DCF model suggests that the stock is almost 40% undervalued, which makes it a 'Strong Buy' again.

Fundamental analysis

Before I delve into my expectations around the upcoming earnings release, let me remind readers that CLS is enjoying the AI race among cloud companies and has demonstrated a strong revenue and EBITDA growth so far this year.

According to SA, Celestica will release its Q3 earnings on October 23 after the market close. Since Celestica was one of the stocks that rallied significantly together with other companies with AI exposure, we need to look at developments around the data center industry. According to delloro.com, 1H2024 worldwide data center capital expenditure grew by 38% on a YoY basis.

There are a few more solid indications that the AI momentum for data centers is not cooling down. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), has recently released its Q3 2024 earnings. TSM's revenue grew by an impressive 36% YoY, and management's Q4 guidance was notably higher than what consensus expected. If TSM achieves its Q4 revenue guidance of $26.5 billion (midpoint of the range), it will mean a 30% YoY growth.

Nvidia's CEO, Jensen Huang, recently said that the demand for the company's new Blackwell GPU is "insane". Based on the soaring revenue of TSM, the company that produces Nvidia's chips, the demand for Blackwell is indeed quite strong. According to the source, Blackwell is expected to be notably more expensive than the company's previous generation Hopper GPU. If the demand is indeed "insane", this means that data center owners are ready to pay a premium price to ensure technological edge.

With that being said, the momentum for semiconductors is expected to remain very strong at least through the end of 2024. It is also highly likely that the industry will remain hot at least through 2025 as well. I think that we are already used to headlines that Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOGL) frequently announce their new investments in data centers across the world. The new trend is that asset managers recently also started investing billions in data centers. For example, Blackstone (BX) recently announced that it plans to invest more than $8 billion in data centers in Spain. Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) has recently established a JV that will also invest billions in data centers.

Therefore, AI tailwinds are still very strong, which is beneficial for Celestica. Celestica's accelerated revenue growth starting from FY 2023 suggests a strong ability to capitalize on AI tailwinds. Therefore, it is fair that the trend of consensus EPS revision looks very bullish. Celestica consistently deliver positive earnings surprises, which is another positive factor before the upcoming earnings release.

Valuation analysis

Celestica's forward P/E ratios look quite low because Wall Street analysts expect solid EPS expansion over the next few years. As a result, the FY2026 forward P/E ratio is currently estimated at just 11. In my fundamental analysis, I have explained why CLS is well-positioned to deliver strong EPS expansion in reality. Therefore, CLS looks very attractively valued based on forward P/E ratios.

The discounted cash flow ('DCF') model will help in figuring out the stock's upside potential. Celestica's WACC is around 8%. Revenue assumptions for FYs 2024-2025 are from consensus. For FYs 2026-2028, I project a 50 basis point growth deceleration per year. The TTM FCF margin is 3.2%, which is expected to expand by 25 bps every year. The constant growth rate for terminal value ('TV') calculation is 3%, which I consider fair given Celestica's solid exposure to secular data center tailwinds. According to Seeking Alpha, there are 117.5 million shares outstanding.

According to the DCF, with all aforementioned assumptions incorporated, the stock's fair price is around $81. This represents a 39% upside potential, which I consider compelling, and my assumptions are quite conservative.

Mitigating factors

Buying the stock before the earnings release is risky. The market's reaction can be quite unpredictable. It is common when investors start selling-off the stock after earnings, even if a company delivered a firm positive EPS surprise. For example, CLS reported its Q2 earnings on July 23 with a positive earnings surprise and robust Q3 guidance. Nevertheless, historical price quotes indicate that the stock was -7.7% on July 24 and -4.35% on July 25. Therefore, even if CLS delivers strong Q3 earnings, there is a risk of a temporary pullback.

There is a significant customer concentration risk, according to Celestica's latest annual report. FY 2023 sales from the largest customer represented around 25% of the total. As a result, Celestica's ten largest customers contributed 64% to consolidated sales. High customer concentration is inherently risky, as it makes any company heavily dependent on its partners' decisions and financial strength. Thus, a potential decrease in revenue from one of its largest customers might have a significant adverse effect on the company's P&L.

Conclusion

My previous bullish call aged well, and I expect the updated bullish thesis to be successful as well. Tailwinds are still strong and there are no signs that the AI momentum is cooling down. Celestica has been quite strong in capitalizing on tailwinds, and there is still solid upside left.

data.title: Our Top 10 Dividend Growth Stocks - October 2024
data.description:
Note: Please note that the stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article (for example, Selection methodology/process, Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks, etc.) will be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them.

Introduction:

This series of articles is for readers who are in the accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they're not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our self-developed and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hyper-growth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks.

Even though we publish this series on a monthly basis, some of the stocks get repeated from one month to another. However, some new ones get added, while some old ones get dropped. We also outline in a later section how best to develop a portfolio based on this series.

Why High-Growth Dividend Stocks?

There are two types of dividend stocks that a DGI investor can choose from depending upon their individual situation, goals, and investing time horizon:

High Growth Low Yield [HGLY]

Low Growth High Yield [LGHY].

As the names suggest, the HGLY category would have stocks that offer a high rate of dividend growth but usually a low current yield. These stocks would normally have low payment ratios, manageable levels of debt, and rapidly rising earnings.

On the other hand, LGHY-type stocks would offer a high current yield (generally 3% and higher) but a lower rate of dividend growth. Generally speaking, these companies are more mature and stable businesses that have their hyper-growth period in the rearview but still grow modestly over time to support a low but stable growth in dividends.

Obviously, there will be stocks that fit somewhere in between these two categories, for example, medium growth and medium current yield.

So, who should own HGLY-type stocks? Basically, anyone who is in the accumulation phase and does not need the income currently and/or in the next five to 10 years should own some high-growth dividend stocks. In addition, folks (including retirees) with large investment capital that generates more income than they need (for example, 1.5x or 2x their income needs) should invest in HGLY-type stocks, at least partially.

How To Structure A Portfolio Based On This Series

Though it would depend a great deal on your personal goals, risk tolerance, investment methodology, and choices if you wish to make a portfolio based on this monthly series, here is one way to do it

  • Make a portfolio budget and provision to have a maximum of 20 stocks over time.
  • Divide your capital (current + future) into 20 equal parts.
  • In the first month, buy five to six positions based on the 10 top stocks for that month.
  • From the next month onwards, check for the new stocks appearing in the top 10 list that are not part of your portfolio, and add new positions (possibly no more than two a month or as many as your process and budget allow).
  • Repeat step 4 until you reach 20 (or max) positions.

When you have reached the maximum 20 positions and have no more capital to add, look for new stocks in our monthly report or based on your own research. If you decide to add a position, you need to find a position you would like to drop and replace it with a new one.

It may also be recommended to monitor your positions periodically, preferably monthly, but at least quarterly. Also, make sure that you do not overweight any particular sector or industry segment.

Selection Criteria

We will draw upon our original current month's data set, taken from our other DGI series (5 Relatively Safe and Cheap DGI), and update it with the current data. We will then apply additional criteria to filter out stocks that have provided a high rate of dividend growth in the recent past and are likely to continue on that path for the foreseeable future.

Please see our original article to get a complete spreadsheet of this dataset. For the sake of clarity, we will list the original filtering criteria below:

  • Market cap > $10 billion (some exceptions can be made)
  • Dividend yield > 1.0% (some exceptions are made to include high quality but lower yielding companies)
  • Daily average volume > 100,000
  • Dividend growth past five years >= 0 (we will check for high growth later).
  • Preferably, a minimum of five years of positive dividend growth.

Subsequent to the above filter, we calculated a score (Dividend Safety Quality Score) that was derived based on the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • EPS growth (average of previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-wk high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years

Notes: All tables in this article are created by the author unless explicitly specified. The stock data have been sourced from various sources such as Seeking Alpha, Yahoo Finance, GuruFocus, IBD, and CCC-List (dripinvesting).

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

A vast majority of stocks selected so far have raised their dividend payouts for five years or more. However, some may not have raised consistently but have paid dividends for a long duration and raised them only periodically. However, as an additional criterion, now we will filter out stocks that have increased their dividend payouts (average of 5-year and 10-year) by an average annual rate of 8% or more (some exceptions are made if the Chowder number is decent). We will also consider stocks that may not have provided a consistent yearly increase but have provided a cumulative increase of 30% in payouts in the last five years.

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 80%.
  • 5-year dividend growth is at least 7.5% or greater. This is in line with the growth rate of the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares (VIG). However, if the five-year growth rate is less than 7.5%, but the 10-year dividend growth rate is greater than 7.5%, we will keep it.
  • Chowder number (five-year dividend growth plus the dividend yield) >= 9. The Chowder number is the sum of the current yield and the dividend growth rate of the last five years. However, if the 5-YR dividend growth rate is less than 7.5%, we will check if the 10-YR growth rate is 7.5% or higher.

After we applied these criteria, we were left with close to 300 stocks on our list. Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends for long. Sure, some companies may try to do it by taking on more debt, cutting costs, or spending less on R&D and capital, but such measures cannot be sustained long before they start causing wider issues. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column a High-Growth Quality-Score [HGQS].

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

From the above list of roughly 300 stocks, we will select roughly 45 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • Top 15 stocks based on the highest HG-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration).
  • Top 10 stocks based on the highest five-year dividend growth.
  • Top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth.
  • Top 10 stocks based on average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription is required).

Table-1A: Top 15 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now remove the duplicates from this list as many stocks qualify based on multiple criteria.

Appeared two times: CI, CSL, FIX, TSM, VOYA (5 duplicates).

We are now left with 40 (45 - 5) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes VMC and QSR (leaving us 38 names).

We will also remove any names that have both the EPS and RS less than 80. This removes TGT, leaving us 37 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. This removes MSFT, FANG, and AMH, leaving us with 34 names.

Finally, we will remove some stocks to avoid over-concentration from one sector or industry segment. We prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Tech-Semiconductor: We keep TSM and MPWR but remove AVGO, QCOM, and KLAC.

Finance-Regional Banks: We keep WAL but remove ZION.

Finance-Prop & Casualty Ins: We keep ERIE but remove TRV.

Finance-Investment Banking: We keep GS and HLNE but remove MS, SF, ARES, and AMP.

Finance-Services: We remove VOYA.

Home-Building: We keep PHM and DHI but remove LEN.

We also remove some of the stocks that have the lowest relative strength in the list. So, we remove CTVA, SBAC, and RGLD. We are now left with 20 names.

In all, we removed 14 names, so we are finally left with 20 (34-14) names, which are presented in the table below:

(FIX), (BAC), (WAL), (PHM), (DHI), (PWR), (CSL), (TTEK), (GS), (HLNE), (CI), (BRO), (ERIE), (RMD), (WING), (DKS), (MPWR), (TSM), (CTAS), and (NRG).

Table 2: Top 20 High Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, some analysis and judgment were used to avoid the concentration of any one sector or industry segment. However, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just 10 stocks is mostly a subjective process. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

To start with, we will sort out our list of the above 20 stocks based on sector-industry segment and then in descending order of net quality score.

We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom. However, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to select either the top (or the second one) from each industry segment. While making the final selections, we also keep in view that this is not a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, so we want to give more weightage to stocks that are likely to do well on a short to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

In this list, we do not give much weightage to how a stock is priced currently (in terms of valuation) or the current yield since many of these high-growth stocks may not trade at cheap valuations.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (October 2024):

(FIX), (PHM), (PWR), (CSL), (HLNE), (RMD), (DKS), (TSM), (CTAS), and (NRG).

Previous Month Selections:

((FIX)), (DHI), (CSL), (AXP), (ALL), (LLY), ((DKS)), ((MPWR)), ((CTAS)), and ((NRG)).

Since our methodology is based largely on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a few months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another month, but it can really vary. We do not change stocks just for the sake of changing.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a "hold" (if not a "buy"). However, for new selections, we take into account many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Past Performance

Now, let's see how our model portfolio of these 10 high-growth stocks would have performed compared to the benchmark Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF or the S&P 500 (SP500) from the beginning of 2008 until the last month. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year. Please remember that this is a back-tested performance, not an actual one. It's almost certain that we would not have picked the same set 17 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns. Please note that one of the stocks, HLNE, was excluded from the back-test as its history only goes back to 2018.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

Since the inception of this series, we have provided the progress and performance of the series. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), and also with the S&P 500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of January 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

This month, five stocks have repeated from the previous month. You may have noticed that the home-building stocks have regularly appeared in this list. This month, PulteGroup (PHM) is replacing D.R. Horton (DHI); though DHI is still attractive and meets most of our criteria, it has lost some momentum. Along similar lines, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) from the semiconductor industry is replacing Monolithic Power (MPWR). Three stocks that have repeated almost on a consecutive basis and served this series well are Comfort Systems (FIX), DICK'S Sporting Goods (DKS), and NRG Energy (NRG). Please note that several of our selected stocks have their earnings reports due between now and the next month. So we expect them to have some volatility, though we expect them to continue the momentum.

Please keep in mind that the market has continued to reach new peaks almost on a regular basis and may be due for some correction or at least a pause. The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we can expect them to have high volatility as well, so due diligence is required.

data.title: One Strategy, Three Outcomes? Why NVDX May Be Better
data.description:
For investors looking to gain leveraged exposure to the performance of NVIDIA (NVDA) shares, they must have come across one of these single-stock leveraged ETFs. As of the last count, there are currently three single-stock ETFs based on NVIDIA shares, the GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF (NVDL), the Direxion Daily NVDA Bull 2X Shares ETF (NVDU), and the T-Rex 2X Long NVIDIA Daily Target ETF (BATS:NVDX), all with the same strategy of providing 200% the daily returns of NVDA. Naturally, the next question is, which of these funds should one pick, if one were so inclined?

Fund Overviews

At the basic level, all three funds (collectively called the 'leveraged NVDA ETFs'), the GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF, the Direxion Daily NVDA Bull 2X Shares ETF, and the T-Rex 2X Long NVIDIA Daily Target ETF, have the same basic strategy of providing 200% the daily investment results, before fees and expenses, of the performance of the common shares of NVIDIA Corporation (Figure 1).

The leveraged NVDA ETFs achieve their mandates by striking total return swaps with large investment banks that deliver the daily exposure required. For example, Figure 2 shows the portfolio holdings of the NVDU ETF, which shows NVDU has struck total return swaps with investment banks like Goldman Sachs and Merrill Lynch (Figure 2).

Similarly, the NVDX ETF has a portfolio of total return swaps, although its counterparties are not mentioned in its portfolio holdings (Figure 3).

Leveraged ETFs Have Compounding Risk

Investors should note that leveraged ETFs like NVDL, NVDU, and NVDX are only designed to provide 200% exposure to NVDA on a 1-day basis. For holding periods longer than 1 day, the returns of the leveraged ETFs may differ materially from the expected leveraged returns of the underlying due to 'compounding risk'. For example, according to NVDU's prospectus:

Compounding has a significant impact on funds that are leveraged and that rebalance daily. The impact of compounding becomes more pronounced as volatility and holding periods increase and will impact each shareholder differently depending on the period of time an investment in the Fund is held and the volatility of NVDA during the shareholder’s holding period of an investment in the Fund.

Figure 4 shows the estimated returns of the NVDU ETF depending on the returns of NVIDIA shares and the stock's volatility. According to Direxion's estimates, the NVDU ETF can perform significantly better than NVIDIA's returns if NVDIA's returns are positive and volatility is low (i.e. NVIDIA shares appreciate at a consistent pace for a long time).

However, if returns are volatile, then an investment in NVDU could deliver significantly lower returns than twice the realized returns of NVIDIA shares. For example, at an annualized 75% volatility, an investment in NVDU is expected to return only 45.9%, even when NVDA shares themselves return 60%.

This is because the daily rebalancing causes the NVDU ETF to lose value from 'volatility decay'. For example, assume NVDA shares gained 5% on day 1 and lost 5% on day 2. On a compounded basis, an unlevered investment in NVDA would have lost 0.25% over the 2 days, so twice the 2-day return should be -0.5%.

However, if the fund is levered 200%, then on day 1, the fund gains 10% (2 x 5%) to 110% of the original value. But on day 2, the fund loses 10% (2 x 5%) and falls to 99% of the original investment. This takes the 2-day loss to 1.0% instead of the -0.5% expected.

While the 'volatility decay' may appear small on a day-to-day basis, if compounded over many days, it can become very significant.

Investors considering leveraged ETFs like NVDL, NVDU, and NVDX should make sure they fully understand the tracking error risks involved with leveraged ETFs by consulting these FINRA and SEC warnings.

Leverage Ratios Caused Returns Difference

Back to the premise of this article, "Which of the leveraged NVDA funds should investors pick?" At first glance, the answer appears simple. Since its inception on October 19th, 2023, a naive analysis of historical performance would show that the NVDX ETF has blown away the competition, returning 620% in total returns compared to 441% for NVDU, 516% for NVDL, and 228% for NVDA itself (Figure 5).

However, historical performance is not the whole story. This is because the NVDL and NVDU were traditionally 1.5x levered funds. The two funds increased their leverage factor to 2.0x on January 21, 2024 and April 2, 2024 respectively in order to compete with the upstart NVDX.

Looking at comparable returns after April 2, 2024, the performance of NVDL, NVDU, and NVDX are much more aligned with each other, with NVDL and NVDX returning 81% compared to NVDU returning 86% and NVDA return 54% (Figure 6).

Persistent Differences In Leverage Factor Suggests Poor Execution

Another way to answer the question is to look at the actual trading execution of the funds, to see how closely they adhere to their mandates. For example, if we compare the daily performance of the leveraged NVDA ETFs to the daily performance of NVDA from April 2, 2024 to October 18, 2024, we can see that the median multiplier of all three leveraged funds is 2.0x. This is the expected result (Figure 7).

However, we can also see that on a day-to-day basis, there is quite a bit of variability in the leverage multiple, particularly on low return days when tracking error and execution can lead to dramatic differences in the multiplier. For example, on April 29, 2024, NVDA was nearly unchanged, but NVDL returned 0.1%, NVDU was unchanged, and NVDX declined by 0.1%, leading to leverage factors of 5.3x, 0.7x, and -3.1x respectively.

Over the long run, differences in execution will show up in the average daily leverage factor and standard deviation of the leverage factor. As we can see from the data above, NVDX appears to have the 'best' execution, as its average (arithmetic mean) daily leverage factor is 2.01 compared to 2.17 for NVDU and 2.22 for NVDL. Furthermore, NVDX has the lowest standard deviation in its leverage factor, with a standard deviation of 1.54 compared to 1.61 for NVDU and 1.64 for NVDL. So NVDX is the 'closest' in achieving its mandate of delivering 200% of the daily performance of NVDA shares.

Negligible Difference In Fees

Finally, considering the fees of the leveraged NVDA ETFs, we can see that the three funds have negligible differences in their expense ratios, with NVDL being the most expensive at 1.15% while NVDU is the cheapest at 1.04% (Figure 8).

Conclusion

NVIDIA bulls have no doubt seen single-stock leveraged ETFs such as NVDL, NVDU, and NVDX that promise 200% of the daily returns of NVIDIA shares. However, which of these funds is the best, for those who want to obtain leveraged returns to NVIDA shares?

In my opinion, the T-REX 2X Long NVIDIA Daily Target ETF appears to have the best execution out of the three funds. The NVDX has modest management fees of 1.05% and has been the most consistent in keeping its leverage ratio at 2.0x. For NVIDIA bulls, I would recommend they choose the NVDX ETF.

data.title: Capital Southwest: 10% Yield, Market Favorite
data.description:
Rising interest rates benefited business development companies greatly over the past several quarters due to the fact that the overwhelming majority of their debt investments were at floating rates, while much of their debt was at fixed rates.

Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC), a BDC founded in 1961, saw its total investment income soar 49% in its fiscal year 2023, which ended on 3/31/24.

Although CSWC's interest expense rose 49%, net investment income, "NII," grew 61%, while NII/share grew ~18%, with the share count rising 35.7%.

As seen in many BDCs in 2023, realized gains were negative - for CSWC they rose ~$23M, to ~-$40M, as several portfolio companies faced difficulties with the higher interest rates.

Fiscal Q1 '25 (period ending 6/30/24) saw a continuation of revenue and NII growth, albeit at a slower pace. The exception was NII/share which fell 3% due to 21.5% growth in the share count, as CSWC's management took advantage of higher prices per share to raise cash.

Realized gains swung from a $12.78M loss in the same period the previous year, to a $0.71M gain, whereas unrealized gains reversed course and turned negative, going from a $12M gain to -$15.54M.

Company Profile:

Capital Southwest Corporation is an internally managed business development company specializing in credit and private equity and venture capital investments in middle market companies, mezzanine, later stage, mature, late venture, emerging growth, buyouts, recapitalizations and growth capital investments. (CSWC site)

Holdings:

As of 6/30/24, CSWC held investments in 117 companies. Its debt investments averaged $12.7M, while its equity holdings averaged $1.925M. The portfolio was split 88.9% in 1st Lien investments, 1.9% in 2nd Lien, and 9.1% in equity investments.

The weighted average yield on debt investments was steady, at 13.3%, while the average yield on total investments rose from 12.7% to 13.5%. The held companies had an average of $19.7M, with average leverage of 3.8X:

Over the past year, ended 6/30/24, management has expanded CSWC's exposure to Healthcare Services, from 11% to 13%. The biggest exposure reduction was in Business Services, which went from 13% to 7%.

Management has also decreased the exposure to Media & Marketing from 13% to 9%. Food, Agriculture & Beverage increased from 5% to 9%.

Portfolio Companies Ratings:

On the flip side of rising rates, one must look at the health of the underlying companies that a BDC holds, especially in light of rising interest rates. This is done on a quarterly basis. CSWC uses a 4-tier system, with 1 being the top tier, and 4 being the lowest.

There were three loans upgraded to tier 1 in fiscal Q1 '24 (period ended 6/30/24), valued at $17.1M, while there were two companies downgraded to tier 3, at $33.5M, and one downgrade to tier 4, at $1.5M.

Tier 3 holdings increased $31M, rising from 5.1% in the previous quarter, to $99.7M in the period ended 6/30/24, while tier 4 holdings decreased by $1M, to $1.9M, as of 6/30/24. The overall rating went from 1.95 to 1.98.

New Business:

CSWC had $108M of originations in fiscal Q1 '25, consisting of investments in three new portfolio companies totaling $49.3M and add-on commitments in 11 portfolio companies totaling $58.8M.

During the quarter, CSWC received full prepayments on eight debt investments totaling $77.2M, for an average return of 12.6%:

Dividends:

Management raised the regular quarterly dividend from $.57 to $.58 and paid another $.06 supplemental dividend for its September payout, marking the fifth straight quarter of $.06 supplemental dividends.

At its 10/17/24 $25.70 price/share, CSWC's base dividend yield was ~9%, with another ~1% coming from the supplemental, for a total dividend yield of 9.96%. As of the end of the quarter, management estimated that CSWC's undistributed taxable income was $0.50/share.

CSWC's coverage factor for the fiscal year ended 3/31/24 was 1.09X.

As of 9/30/15, CSWC's NAV/Share was $17.68, vs. $16.60 as of 6/30/24.

However, it has paid out $12.84 in regular dividends and $3.95 in supplemental dividends over this period, creating overall value of $33.39/share:

Insiders:

Two directors continued to buy shares in August-September, a total of 656, at prices ranging from $23.94 to $25.03.

Profitability and Leverage:

ROA and ROE continued to improve in fiscal 2024, (period ending 3/31/24), and in fiscal Q1 '25, (period ending 6/30/24), remaining well above BDC industry averages. EBIT margin recovered in fiscal Q1 '25, and was above the industry average.

CSWC's leverage grew more conservative over the past five quarters - at 0.95X. As of 6/30/24, it was much lower than the 1.09X average for the BDC industry.

CSWC's 2.98X EBIT/Interest coverage was in line with its industry's average.

Debt and Liquidity:

CSWC's next debt maturity isn't until January 2026, when $140M in 4.5% Notes come due. Its credit facilities don't mature until 2028 - 2029.

Performance:

CSWC has greatly outperformed its industry over the past year, rising 17% in price, plus a dividend yield of ~10%, for ~total one-year return of ~27%, vs. the BDC industry's average return of 15.85%. It has also gained 9.37% in price so far in 2024, vs. a flat performance from the BDC industry.

CSWC has lagged the broad financial sector, and the S&P 500 over the past year and so far in 2024. The expectation for lowering interest rates has pressured BDC's share price in 2024.

Analysts' Price Targets:

At $25.70, CSWC was already 1% higher than Wall Street analysts' average price target of $25.42, and 11.4% lower than their $29.00 highest target.

Valuations:

At $25.70, CSWC is trading at a big ~55% premium to its 6/30/24 NAV/share of $16.60. In fact, it has the third highest premium to NAV in the BDC industry, which has an average price to NAV of 1%.

On an earnings basis, CSWC's trailing price/NII of 9.59X is also higher than its industry's average, as is its forward P/E of 9.55, and its EV/EBIT:

Parting Thoughts:

We rate CSWC a HOLD for income, and we don't advise buying new shares at its present high premium valuation.

CSWC will release its second quarter 2025 results on Monday, Oct. 28, 2024, after the market closes.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: Week Ahead: Is The Closeness Of The U.S. Election A Source Of Dollar Demand?
data.description:
The dollar rose against all the G10 currencies last week, but it was not because of higher US rates. In fact, the 10-year US Treasury yield fell for the first time in five weeks. The two-year yield did not rise for the first time in three weeks. Rather than an increase in US rates, several other countries' rates fell. The result was that the US 2-year premium over Germany rose for the fourth consecutive week and is now the most since June. The US premium over Canada rose for the third week and the sixth week in the past seven. It is approaching 100 bp, the most since 1997. The US discount to the UK narrowed to less than five basis points, the least in nearly two months. Both the J.P. Morgan and the MSCI emerging market currency indices fell for the third consecutive week.

The US election is emerging as a factor. The tightness of the contest at this late date is maximum uncertainty. In the capital markets, volatility is risk. In politics, risk is a function of credibility (how likely something is) and capability (how powerful it may be). In this framing, the political risk has focused on a Trump victory, whose aggressive tariff proposal are thought to be dollar positive in the first instance because of the across-the-board tariffs, the defection of the US from the managed free-trade world that it was instrumental in building is seen as negative shock to US trading partners. The debate seems to be over how big of a shock and the extent of the retaliation. In the week ahead, however, there are four highlights: China will likely cut loan prime rates before the market open Monday; the Bank of Canada is likely to deliver a 50 bp rate cut following the sub-2% inflation reading; the Fed's Beige Book, which Federal Reserve Chair Powell has put emphasis on, especially at turning points, as was the case last month; and the preliminary October PMI.

United States: There are two types of data in the coming days: Survey data for October (various Fed surveys and the preliminary PMI) and some late Q3 real sector data (new and existing homes sales, durable and factory orders). The first estimate for Q3 GDP is due October 30 and this week's data are unlikely to have much impact on expectations. The Atlanta Fed's GDP tracker for Q3 GDP is 3.4% and NY Fed's model puts it at 3%, the same as the median in Bloomberg's latest survey. Arguably, the Fed's Beige Book is the most important report next week. Past Fed chairs have tended to play down the Summary of Economic Projections and rarely mentioned the Beige Book. Chair Powell takes a different tack. He plays up the dot plot and has cited the Beige Book at turning points. He mentioned it most recently to help justify the half-point rate cut that kicked off the easing cycle in September. Looking a bit further down the field, but not wanting to get too far ahead of ourselves, we continue to think that the market exaggerated the significance of the September employment report. The early forecasts for October nonfarm payrolls (November 1) are coming in around 130k.

The Dollar Index's upside correction has been a little deeper than we expected, encouraged by the market's re-convergence with the Fed's forward guidance, which resulted in a recovery in short-term US rates. This is the fourth rally of the year. The first one lasted about the first six weeks of the year and was worth almost 4%. The second one was from early March through mid-April. It was worth a little more than 4%. The third one lasted about three weeks in June, and the Dollar Index rose by slightly more than 2%. The fourth rally began in late September, and through last week's high, appreciated by about 3.7%. It stopped shy of the (61.8%) retracement of the decline since the high for the year was recorded in mid-April, found near 104.10. The momentum indicators are stretched and look poised to turn lower shortly. Initial support may be around 103.00 and then 102.45.

China: Beijing could continue to drip-feed policy adjustments to the market. Yet, the one-year Medium-Term Lending Facility has been downgraded as a policy instrument and could very well remain at 2%, after last month's 30 bp cut (followed a 20 bp cut in July). However, the loan prime rates have not been cut since July and a 20 bp cut seems likely before the markets open on Monday. A 20 bp cut would bring the one-year rate to 3.15% and the five-year rate to 3.65%. However, when last week's fiscal briefing came up short on specifics, attention shifted to the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress meeting (October 20-24). China's critics want to see fiscal support to bolster domestic demand, and in particular, consumption. There is also a BRICS Summit (October 22-24). In some ways, it is repeating the experience of the EU, pursing a broadening strategy over deepening. The more countries join, the wider range of interests, the more difficult to arrive at timely decisions. There are two contradictions within BRICS. First, at the very heart is an antagonism between India and China. The second is Beijing’s effort to internationalize the role of the yuan and promote its China International Payment System (CIPS). Why would it support the development of a rival platform or currency? The BRICS could endorse greater use of their own national currencies to invoice and settle trade, and it would not be the first time. There is little formally stopping them, but progress has been very slow, begging the question why.

As the US dollar rebounded broadly since late September, it recovered from about CNH6.97 to reach almost CNH7.15 last week. A move above there would target the CNH7.18 area and the 200-day moving average near CNH7.2035. Previous resistance around CNH7.10 may now act as support, and then CNH7.08. Due to idiosyncratic developments in both Japan and China, the correlation of the respective currencies weakened in recent week but has stabilized and looks to improve.

Japan: There is practically no chance that the Bank of Japan changes policy at its October 30-31 meeting. The national election is on October 27, and BOJ officials have done little to prepare the market. After the drama around the July move, it can be expected to tread carefully and communicate clearly. While Japan sees the preliminary October PMI and the final estimate of September machine tool orders (preliminary estimate -6.5% year-over-year), the most important data point is Tokyo's October CPI. It provides a robust guide to the national figure, which is not released for several weeks. In fact, the national September figures were reported last week, confirming the decline Tokyo pointed to in both the headline and core measures. Tokyo's core CPI (excludes fresh food) peaked in January 2023 at 4.3%. It was halved before the end of last year (2.1% year-over-year in December 2023). It has averaged 2.1% in the first nine months of the year and was at 2.0% in September.

Still, we note that changes in the exchange rate is more correlated with changes in the US 10-year yields than with changes in the 10-year differential (~0.72 vs. 0.54) on a 60-day rolling basis. The correlation of changes in the exchange rate and the US 10-year yields is the highest since the spike in early 2020. The 60-day correlation on changes in the 10-year interest rate spread, and the exchange rate peaked slightly above 0.70 in February and fell to the low of the year near 0.32 in August before recovering. The US 10-year yield appears to be stalling around 4.10%, and that warns that the greenback may struggle to extend to its gains that carried it to about JPY150.30 in the second half of last week. The momentum indicators look poised to turn down. Initial support is seen near JPY148.85.

Eurozone: Last week's ECB rate cut may reduce the market's sensitivity to the preliminary October PMI. A poor number will lend credence to market expectations of another cut at the next ECB meeting (December 12). With September CPI below 2%, the ECB has more leeway to act, and the central bank's chief economist Lane has already warned of an accelerated easing cycle if the economy disappoints. Germany is fragile. The economy is on a knife's edge: It might be contracting for the second consecutive year. It would be for the first time since 2002-2003. The IFO survey will be updated October 25. Expectations have fallen for the past four months. The current assessment has not risen in April, and at 84.4 in September, it was lowest since mid-2020. Note that Moody's is to announce the results of its review of France's creditworthiness on October 25. Moody's currently sees France as an Aa2 credit, while Fitch and S&P are one notch lower (AA-). Since late July, the French 10-year premium over Germany has traded mostly 70-80 bp. In Q1 24, the premium was more like 45-55 bp. France's 10-year yield is above Spain and Portugal's.

The euro's pullback has been a little deeper than we expected. The double top near $1.12 and neckline near $1.10 projected to $1.08, but we thought this was a bit deep. Yet, the low set last Thursday was about $1.0810. It stabilized ahead of the weekend, but must recover above $1.0870, and ideally, $1.09 to be notable from a technical perspective. Weighing on the euro has been a surge in the US two-year premium over Germany. The premium fell to almost 135 bp in mid-September, the smallest since May 2023 and recovered to nearly 190 bp before the weekend, the most since late June. The spread reflects the divergence in economic performances and adjustment to central bank outlooks. If the interest rate adjustment is over or nearly so, the euro may begin finding better traction.

UK: The softer September CPI lends credence to the market's conviction that the Bank of England will deliver its second quarter-point cut when it meets again on November 7. There was over a 90% chance priced into the overnight swaps before the CPI report. The real question is about the last meeting of the year (December 19). The market was split, but after the CPI, the swaps market's confidence of a cut in December increased from a little less than a 50% probability to around 75% and was little changed after the stronger-than-expected September retail sales report. The CBI trends and preliminary PMI, due in the coming days, are unlikely to have a significant impact. With the jobs report, CPI, and retail sales behind it, the market now has little distraction from the first Labour budget, after a rocky first 100-days or so. Chancellor of the Exchequer Reeves discovered a GBP22 bln funding gap and seeks to close it, but in the campaign, Labour ruled out increasing the VAT, employee contributions to the NIH (National Institutes of Health), and income tax. Of course, there are other taxes that can be raised, including the capital gain tax, which is at the low range of income tax rates, business contributions to NIH, resident foreigners, and tightening the carried interest tax break. However, it will challenge Prime Minister Starmer's efforts to re-brand Labour as the party for business.

Sterling traded below $1.30 for the first time last week in nearly two months. It held slightly above $1.2960, the (61.8%) retracement target of the rally from the August 8 low near $1.2665. Sterling recovered to about $1.3070 after the stronger retail sales report before the weekend. A close above $1.31 bolster the chances a low is in place. The euro set a new 2 1/2-year low against sterling ahead of the weekend, slightly below GBP0.8300. It recovered to set new session highs in North America near GBP0.8335. The euro must overcome resistance in the GBP0.8350-80 area to boost the technical tone.

Canada: The Bank of Canada meets on October 23. It has cut rates three times this year, and Governor Macklem has suggested that there may be scope to accelerate the pace. Last week's CPI underscored that such scope may very well exist. Headline inflation fell to 1.6% in the year through September, the lowest since February 2021. It has fallen on a monthly basis in three of the past four months, during which time it has contracted by almost 1% at an annualized rate. The underlying core measures, which the central bank emphasizes but does not formally target it, are stickier, but trend, which is important, remains lower, though the median and trimmed mean calculation core CPI. The swaps market is pricing in around an 85% chance that the Bank of Canada delivers a 50 bp cut, up from about 55% chance a week ago. Moreover, the market is beginning to consider another 50 bp cut at the last meeting of the year in December. If one subtracts current CPI from the overnight target, a proxy for the real policy rate, Canada and US rates are roughly the same, around 2.60%-2.65%. But that does not get you far in understanding the run on the Canadian dollar. Instead, note Canada's nominal two-year yield is nearly 100 bp less than what the US Treasury offers. The discount widened around 35 bp since earlier this month and is the largest discount since the 1997.

There has been a dramatic run on the Canadian dollar this month. It has risen only three sessions in the past three weeks, and two of them were last week. The greenback reached a two-month high near CAD1.3840. And yet, the Canadian dollar is the best-performing G10 currency this month, down only 2%. A break of the CAD1.3750-CAD1.3850 may signal the direction of the next big figure move.

Australia: The stronger-than-expected September employment report further dampened market speculation of a rate cut this year. Australia has filled 290k full-time positions in the first nine months of the year, compared with 143k in the same period last year. The participation rate reached a new record high of 67.2% (66.5% in September 2023), while the unemployment rate was steady at 4.1% (which it has averaged since the end of Q1). The futures market recognizes less than a 7% chance of a rate cut next month. The odds of a December cut are about 20%, the least since the end of July. Australia sees the preliminary October PMI on October 24, but it tends not to be a market-mover. Instead, the quarterly CPI on October 30 is the next important data point for the Reserve Bank and the markets.

The Australian dollar is also sensitive to developments in China, directly as its largest trading partner, and indirectly, through commodity prices and the terms of trade. The Aussie found support last week slightly below $0.6660, coming close to the (50%) retracement of the rally from the low for the year set in early August (~$0.6350) to the year's high recorded at the end of September (~$0.6940). The momentum indicators have not turned up, but the Australian dollar posted a four-day high close ahead of the weekend. A move above $0.6720 would be constructive, but the $0.6760 area offers more formidable resistance.

Mexico: Mexico is particularly vulnerable if Trump is re-elected. Mexico's modernization, to a large extent, has been driven by the integration into the continental economy with the US and Canada. Trump has suggested 60% tariffs on China and 20% on goods from the rest of the world. He has singled out the auto sector and says that automakers building plants in Mexico are a "serious threat" to the US. Mexico exports about 85% of its vehicle production (mostly to the US). Depending on the extent of the retaliation, some estimates project that "Trump tariffs" could cost Mexico 2% of GDP by 2028. Investors have yet to be convinced that the new government will be any more friendly than the previous government, while moderating price pressures will allow the central bank to cut rates again before the end of the year.

The US dollar rallied from almost MXN19.24 at the start of the last week to poke above MXN20.00 last Thursday for the first time in a little more than a month. It reversed low and posted a bearish shooting star candlestick. Follow-through dollar selling ahead of the weekend saw a three-day low near MXN19.65 before snapping back to MXN19.90 in late dealings. Political considerations warn of the continued downside risks for the peso ahead of the US election. The US dollar is likely to hold above MXN19.50 and last month's high, almost MXN20.15, will likely be challenged. The greenback is also pushing against the upper end of a two-month range against the Brazilian real near BRL5.70. The high for the year was set in early August near BRL5.8550.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: REITs Rally As Earnings Begin
data.description:
Real Estate Weekly Outlook

U.S. equity markets extended their winning streak into a sixth week - while benchmark interest rates posted weekly declines for the first time since the September rate cut - as a sharp retreat in oil prices calmed near-term inflation worries, while a busy slate of corporate earnings results and economic data were broadly consistent with a continuing "soft landing" economic trajectory.

Closing the week at fresh record-highs, the S&P 500 gained another 0.9% this week - posting gains for the eleventh week in the past twelve. The "value rotation" theme was back this week after a month-long reversal, with gains led by rate-sensitive segments and smaller-cap domestic companies - the laggards throughout the Fed's hiking cycle. The Small-Cap 600 rallied 1.7% on the week, while the Mid-Cap 400 gained 1.4%, each significantly outpacing the 0.2% gains on the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100. Real estate equities were notable leaders this week - snapping a five-week streak of underperformance - buoyed by a solid start to earnings season and a slight pull-back in benchmark rates. The Equity REIT Index rallied 2.9% this week, with 16-of-18 property sectors in positive territory. The Mortgage REIT Index gained 3.1%, while Homebuilders surged nearly 6%.

Helping to ease some concerns of firming inflation on the heels of lukewarm CPI and PPI data last week, WTI Crude Oil plunged nearly 8% this week - dipping back below $70/barrel - on a combination of weak European and Chinese economic data and indications of modestly easing tensions in the Middle East. After jumping above $77 in early October, oil prices are again trading negative on the year and are back within 5% of three-year lows. Soft trends across Europe and Asia have helped to contain some of the interest rate uplift over the past month from a brighter outlook on the U.S. consumer sparked by a series of solid employment reports. Retreating from its highest levels since July 31st, the 10-Year Treasury Yield declined 3 basis points on the week to 4.08% - up from its mid-September lows of 3.62% but still down from its 2024 highs of 4.71%. The policy-sensitive 2-Year Treasury Yield declined by 1 basis point to 3.95%. Swaps markets now price-in 0.90 rate cuts in November (unchanged from last week) and project a total of 3.75 rate cuts in 2024 (including the 2 in September) - down slightly from 3.84 last Friday.

Real Estate Economic Data

Below, we recap the most important macroeconomic data points over this past week affecting the residential and commercial real estate marketplace.

Consistent with the theme of the past several months, economic data and corporate news flow this past week lacked a clear-cut narrative, revealing ongoing pockets of softness in manufacturing and housing market activity, but also notable resilience in labor market trends and consumer activity. A key look into the health and sentiment of the U.S. consumer, Retail Sales were marginally better than expected in September, highlighted by the strongest Core Retail Sales (ex-Auto/Gas) figure in three months. Total Retail Sales rose 0.4% during the month and 1.7% from a year earlier, topping estimates of 0.3% and 1.6%, respectively. Ten of the report's 13 categories posted increases - led by clothing, health/personal care, and grocery stores - which offset a drag from lower gasoline and auto sales. Employment data was solid as well, as closely watched weekly Jobless Claims figures showed a pullback in initial claims following a Hurricane-related surge in the prior week. Manufacturing data was notably soft, however, with Industrial Production declining in September amid headwinds from a series of labor union strikes.

Equity REIT & Homebuilder Week In Review

Best & Worst Performance This Week Across the REIT Sector

Office: An office revival? The top-performing property sector this year with gains of nearly 40%, office REITs extended their rebound this week as the initial slate of earnings results showed a continued recovery in office space demand. NYC-focused SL Green (SLG) - the fourth-largest office REIT - surged more than 16% this week after it kicked off office REIT earnings season with a solid report highlighted by impressive leasing activity. SLG signed 764k SF of Manhattan office space in the third quarter - its largest quarterly activity in two years - which was roughly 40% above its average pre-pandemic level from 2017-2019. Just as impressive, SLG signed these leases at rents that were 10.8% higher than its prior fully escalated rents on the same space - its second-straight quarter of double-digit leasing spreads. The momentum continued into the third quarter as well: SLG announced that it signed a massive 925k SF renewal and expansion lease with Bloomberg, LP at 919 Third Avenue. The deal includes an 11-year lease covering 749k SF in its existing footprint and a 15-year lease covering a 175k SF expansion. As a result of this activity, SLG significantly boosted its full-year guidance for leasing activity to 3.3M SF - up from its prior target of 2.0M - and increased its occupancy target to 92.5% - up from 91.6% previously.

Elsewhere in the office space, Sunbelt-focused Piedmont (PDM) rallied nearly 12% this week after it provided preliminary third-quarter operating metrics showing a continued recovery in office demand. PDM signed 461k sf of leasing during the third quarter of 2024 - a slowdown from the impressive Q2 bookings of over 1M SF - but still a decent haul that is within 10% of its pre-pandemic averages. This leasing activity helped to raise its in-service leased percentage to 88.8%, up from 87.3% at the end of Q2. PDM noted that its contractual backlog stands at 1.5 million square feet of leased space yet to commence, representing approximately $48 million of future annual cash flow. As noted in our Earnings Preview, brokerage firm JLL (JLL) released its quarterly office update last week, which showed very encouraging trends for the long-struggling office sector. Nationally, third-quarter leasing activity maintained strong momentum from Q2, growing 0.4% quarter-over-quarter to 50.4M SF. Leasing over the past six months reflects 86% of pre-pandemic activity levels. JLL reports that office market availability declined for the first time in over five years - an important milestone - citing a concurrent acceleration in leasing activity and the slowdown of new supply.

Industrial: We saw results from a trio of industrial REITs this week, which painted a mixed picture regarding the state of logistics-related space demand, indicating some continued near-term softening in demand and lingering supply headwinds - but not the sharp, widespread drop-off that some had feared. Prologis (PLD) - the largest logistics property owner in the nation - kicked off earnings season with a surprisingly solid report - and Sunbelt-focused First Industrial (FR) reported even stronger trends - but West Coast-focused Rexford (REXR) followed with a downbeat report later in the week. Sector stalwart Prologis gained 2% after it maintained its property-level metrics and marginally raised its outlook for full-year Core FFO - now expecting 8.0% growth this year. PLD reported solid leasing activity, achieving an impressive cash leasing spread of 44.1% - down slightly from 51.4% in Q2 - while noting that much of this rent slowdown is attributable to weakness in Southern California. Rexford - which focuses exclusively on this region - dipped 3% on the week after lowering its full-year NOI growth outlook and noted that SoCal market rents fell another 2.5% during the quarter and were roughly 7.5% lower from a year earlier, which resulted in a sharp cooldown in rent spreads to 26.7% in Q3 from 49.0% last quarter. REXR cited "increased levels of global unrest, uncertainty related to the presidential election, and an uncertain economic outlook" in driving a "slower" pace of activity.

Sunbelt-focused First Industrial gained 1.5% after reporting decent results - raising its full-year NOI and FFO outlook driven by impressive rent spreads, but trimmed its occupancy outlook following an unexpected vacancy of a 1.1M SF facility in Pennsylvania leased to U.K.-based retailer Boohoo. Underscoring the continued embedded upside from mark-to-market rent adjustments, FR achieved impressive cash rent spreads of 66.8% - its second-best quarter on record and up from 43% last quarter - and FR now expects full-year FFO growth of 7.8% - up from 7.0% previously. Macro commentary was relatively upbeat, conceding that activity-levels nationwide have clearly softened from the robust pace from 2021-2023, but FR expects demand to pick-up after the "fog" from election uncertainty clears. FR noted that overall market rents are "up 2-3%" excluding Southern California, and "flattish to up a little bit" overall. We heard similar commentary from Prologis, which reiterated its call that fundamentals are "bottoming," and provided updated metrics that reflected expectations of soft fundamentals in the near-term followed by a recovery in 2025 as supply growth cools. Prologis slightly downgraded its net absorption forecast this year to 160M SF - down from 165M last quarter - and revised up its supply forecast to 300M - up from 290M. Prologis now expects a market-wide vacancy rate of 6.8% at the end of 2024 - up from 6.6% previously.

Cell Tower: Crown Castle (CCI) - the second-largest cell tower REIT - gained 1.5% this week after it reported in-line results and maintained its full-year outlook, but provided few incremental details on a rumored deal to sell its non-tower assets. Earlier this month, Reuters reported that CCI was nearing a potential deal to sell its fiber unit and potentially also its "small cell" unit, noting that Zayo Group and private equity firm TPG (TPG) are the final bidders. Crown Castle had expanded its fiber business through a series of acquisitions beginning in 2015, but the capital-intensive nature of building fiber infrastructure combined with its more-tower-focused peer's stock price outperformance has prompted calls from investors and activists to reconsider its strategy and focus on its core macro tower unit. CCI did not comment on reports, but did announce that it trimmed its small-cell deployment plans by 7,000 nodes at "locations that had countless zoning and permitting delays or in high-cost markets that did not meet our investment parameters." CCI still has 40k small-cell locations in its backlog - 70% of which are higher-yield colocation nodes as opposed to single-tenant anchor build. Commentary on carrier demand was status-quo, with CCI reiterating that demand is "steady state" following a significant decline in network spending last year. CCI maintained its outlook, which anticipates comparable revenue growth of 4.5% from towers, 15% from small cells, and 2% from fiber solutions.

Net Lease: Small-cap Alpine Income (PINE) - which owns 137 properties across 34 states - gained 3% this week after it kicked-off net lease earnings season with a solid report. Driven by recent "capital recycling" transactions, PINE now expects full-year AFFO growth of 14.1%, up from 8.7% previously. Consistent with its recent "selective pruning" of its portfolio by selling lower-yielding assets and recycling the proceeds into higher-yield assets, PINE noted that it acquired four net leased retail properties for $37.5M, at a weighted average initial cash cap rate of 8.8%, while selling eight properties for $48.6M at a weighted average exit cash cap rate of 6.8%. PINE noted that it continued to reduce its exposure to Walgreens (WBA) - previously its top tenant at 12% of ABR - which announced earlier this year that it is seeking to "significantly" reduce its store count. PINE sold two Walgreens during the quarter, which dropped its exposure to below 10%. Speaking on the macro environment, PINE remarked that it has seen an uptick in transaction velocity following a two-year lull during the Fed's hiking cycle, but indicated that it sees better risk-reward in the loan origination space - noting that it expects its loan portfolio to be about 10% of its total asset value going forward.

Hotels: On the downside this week, Service Properties (SVC) - one of four public REITs that are externally managed by asset manager RMR Group (RMR) - dipped 25% this week after it joined the four other RMR-advised REITs in slashing its dividend while concurrently announcing a deal to sell a significant slice of its portfolio in order to pay down debt. SVC - which operates with an elevated debt ratio of 82% - reduced its quarterly dividend by 95% to $0.01/share, noting that the dividend reduction will result in $127M of annual savings. SVC also announced plans to sell 114 hotels (14,925 rooms) - over half of its 220-property hotel portfolio - in order to reduce leverage, citing "deteriorating leverage metrics" due to a slow recovery of its hotel portfolio and significant renovation cap-ex needs. The hotels are managed by its RMR-managed affiliate - Sonesta International - and neither the buyer nor the price was disclosed, but SVC noted that the portfolio had $850M in aggregate book value. SVC noted that it had planned to spend $725M in capital expenditures over the next six years at these hotels. Since the end of 2019, the four RMR-advised REITs declined by an average of 75%. Office Properties Income (OPI) - which has dipped 90% since 2019 - slashed its dividend to a penny in January. Industrial Logistics Properties (ILPT) - which has dipped 76% - slashed its dividend to a penny in 2022. Diversified Healthcare (DHC) - which has dipped 52% - slashed its dividend to a penny in 2020.

This week, we published our REIT Earnings Preview, which discussed the major high-level themes and metrics we'll be watching across each property sector. The sector with the most upside from easing interest rates, REITs rebounded sharply ahead of the Fed's "jumbo" rate cut, but have fluctuated in October amid changing rate expectations. The "soft landing" trajectory has been particularly supportive of office and retail REITs - along with small-caps - each aching for a "Goldilocks" environment of lower rates and lukewarm growth. These macroeconomic conditions - combined with rebounding equity valuations - are ideal for a revival of the long-dormant "animal spirits" for public REITs through IPOs and acquisitions of debt-burdened private portfolios. Notable trends gleaned from recent data include: 1) gradually recovering office fundamentals; 2) anchored housing market trends despite historically low activity levels; 3) sustained hotel and leisure demand; 4) cooling in (currently robust) retail fundamentals; and 5) sluggish trends in "goods-oriented" sectors (industrial, farmland, timber). Below, we compiled the earnings calendar for equity REITs and homebuilders.

REIT Capital Raising Activity

It was a quiet week on the capital-raising front following a surge in activity in September and into early October. Community Healthcare (CHCT) amended and extended its $400M credit facility - increasing its total capacity by $150M and lowering its pricing by 10-30 basis points - which it used to pay off a separate $75M term loan facility. On the credit ratings front, a trio of office REITs received credit rating downgrades. S&P Ratings lowered its credit ratings on Kilroy Realty (KRC) to "BBB-" from "BBB" with a stable outlook; S&P lowered its rating on Highwoods (HIW) to "BBB-" from "BBB" with a stable outlook; and S&P lowered its issuer credit rating of Piedmont (PDM) to "BB+" from "BBB-" but affirmed its "BBB-" senior unsecured notes rating with a stable outlook. Meanwhile, S&P affirmed its ratings on Boston Properties (BXP) at "BBB" with a negative outlook. Elsewhere, Fitch Ratings affirmed its credit ratings on American Assets (AAT) at "BBB" with a stable outlook, and Fitch affirmed its credit ratings on Equinix (EQIX) at "BBB" with a stable outlook. Finally, Moody's affirmed the credit ratings of Sabra Health Care (SBRA) at "Ba1" and revised its outlook to positive from stable.

2024 Performance Recap

Performance trends have shifted dramatically over the two months, as the long-underperforming real estate sector has closed some of the historically wide gap with other segments of the equity market amid a sharp pull-back in benchmark interest rates. The Equity REIT Index is now higher by 13.6% this year, while the Mortgage REIT Index is higher by 6.7%. This compares with the 24.2% gain on the S&P 500, the 16.1% gain for the Mid-Cap 400, and the 10.2% gain for the Small-Cap 600. Within the REIT sector, 14 of the 18 property sectors are higher for the year, led by Cannabis, Office, Billboard, and Healthcare REITs, while Farmland, Hotel, and Industrial REITs have lagged on the downside. At 4.08%, the 10-Year Treasury Yield is now higher by 20 basis points on the year, while the 2-Year Treasury Yield has dipped by 48 basis points to 3.95%. The aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index is now higher by 3.0% this year on a total return basis. In commodities markets, WTI Crude Oil is higher by 7.1% this year, while the broader Commodities complex is now higher by 4.2% on the year as higher oil prices have been partially offset by a dip in Natural Gas prices.

Economic Calendar In The Week Ahead

Housing markets will remain the focus of the economic calendar in the week ahead. We'll see both major home sales reports earlier in the week, which will provide good insight into where the housing market stands after two years of abnormally tight monetary policy. On Wednesday, Existing Home Sales data is expected to show an annualized sales velocity of 3.9M in September - still hovering around three-decade lows, and considerably below the typical 5.0-5.5M range. On Thursday, we'll see New Home Sales data, which is expected to show sales activity at a 710k annualized sales pace in September - roughly flat from a year ago - as builders continue to contend with financing and affordability issues. On Thursday, we'll also be watching the weekly Jobless Claims report for indications of a further deterioration in employment trends. On Friday, we'll see the revised October Consumer Sentiment data from the University of Michigan, which unexpectedly dipped in the preliminary report earlier this month amid renewed concerns over inflation and the economy.
